(born 29 September 1970)[[1]](#cite_note-tribute-1) is a Canadian stand-up comedian, actor, and producer.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He began performing in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") in 1989 and won a [Gemini Award](/wiki/Gemini_Award "Gemini Award") in 2008. In 2013, he was number three on *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*' [list of the world's highest-paid comedians](/wiki/Forbes_Celebrity_100 "Forbes Celebrity 100"), and became the first comedian to get a [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") stand-up special.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He also won the [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award") and the [International Emmy Award for Best Arts Programming](/wiki/International_Emmy_Award_for_Best_Arts_Programming "International Emmy Award for Best Arts Programming") for producing *[Hip-Hop Evolution](/wiki/Hip-Hop_Evolution "Hip-Hop Evolution")* (2016). He lives in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[4]](#cite_note-Hough-4)

**Russell Dominic Peters** (born 29 September 1970)[[1]](#cite_note-tribute-1) is a Canadian stand-up comedian, actor, and producer.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He began performing in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") in 1989 and won a [Gemini Award](/wiki/Gemini_Award "Gemini Award") in 2008. In 2013, he was number three on *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*' [list of the world's highest-paid comedians](/wiki/Forbes_Celebrity_100 "Forbes Celebrity 100"), and became the first comedian to get a [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") stand-up special.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He also won the [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award") and the [International Emmy Award for Best Arts Programming](/wiki/International_Emmy_Award_for_Best_Arts_Programming "International Emmy Award for Best Arts Programming") for producing *[Hip-Hop Evolution](/wiki/Hip-Hop_Evolution "Hip-Hop Evolution")* (2016). He lives in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[4]](#cite_note-Hough-4)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Russell Dominic Peters was born on 29 September 1970 in [Toronto, Ontario](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), to immigrant parents from India. His parents Eric and Maureen Peters are of [Anglo-Indian](/wiki/Anglo-Indian "Anglo-Indian") descent, who had moved to Canada in 1965 from [Jalandhar](/wiki/Jalandhar "Jalandhar") and [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") respectively.[[5]](#cite_note-faq-5)[[6]](#cite_note-caden-6) His extended family lives in [Bhopal, India](/wiki/Bhopal "Bhopal").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Peters was raised [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[6]](#cite_note-caden-6)

When Peters was four, he and his family moved to [Brampton](/wiki/Brampton "Brampton"). He attended [Chinguacousy Secondary School](/wiki/Chinguacousy_Secondary_School "Chinguacousy Secondary School") for grades 9–10, and [North Peel Secondary School](/wiki/Judith_Nyman_Secondary_School "Judith Nyman Secondary School") in [Bramalea](/wiki/Bramalea,_Ontario "Bramalea, Ontario") for grades 11–12.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Digitaljournal.com-10) In school, he was regularly bullied [because of his ethnicity](/wiki/Racism "Racism"). He eventually learned [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), which helped him resist the bullying.[[11]](#cite_note-peters-11) Peters also became a fan of [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") in his youth. By the 1990s, he was a well-connected [DJ](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey") in the Toronto scene.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Peters's older brother, Clayton Peters, serves as his manager.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Peters began performing in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") in 1989. He has since gone on to perform in several countries.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1992, Peters met American comedian [George Carlin](/wiki/George_Carlin "George Carlin"), one of his biggest influences, who advised him to get on stage whenever and wherever possible. Peters said he "took that advice to heart, and I think that's the reason I am where I am now."[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 2007, 15 years later, he hosted one of Carlin's last shows before the comedian's death the following year.[[11]](#cite_note-peters-11)

On 28 September 2013, Peters was awarded the 2013 Trailblazer award by the Association of South Asians in Media, Marketing and Entertainment (ASAMME) for good contributions to comedy. He is among the first South Asian Americans to achieve international success in the field.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2017, Peters made an appearance on *[Top Gear America](/wiki/Top_Gear_America "Top Gear America")* in the third episode of season 1 as one of the guests.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

According to *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*, Peters earned an estimated $15 million between June 2009 and June 2010, continuing his run as one of the highest-paid comedians, after earning an estimated $5 million the prior year. *Forbes* ranked him as the seventh-highest-paid comedian.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 2013, he earned $21 million, according to *Forbes*' estimate.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Notable performances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable performances")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/RussellPeters08TIFF.jpg/250px-RussellPeters08TIFF.jpg)](/wiki/File:RussellPeters08TIFF.jpg)

Peters at the [2008 Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/2008_Toronto_International_Film_Festival "2008 Toronto International Film Festival")

#### *Comedy Now!* special

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Comedy Now! special")]

Peters credits the turning point in his career to his 2004 special on the Canadian TV show *[Comedy Now!](/wiki/Comedy_Now! "Comedy Now!")*, which was uploaded onto [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), where it became popular. While the initial video upload featured his entire 45-minute performance, YouTube users subsequently uploaded segments of the performance in which Peters focused on individual cultural groups. According to Peters, those segments were seen by the targeted cultural groups and were well received by them. The video and its viral nature was referred to by Peters on his performance, [*Outsourced*](/wiki/Outsourced_(album) "Outsourced (album)"); when the audience cheered when he referred to earlier jokes. He has also been the one to tell the !Xobile story, and he exclaimed, "Look at you, you filthy downloaders!"[[22]](#cite_note-Russell_Peters:_Outsourced_Aired_on_COMEDYP_—_Ark_TV_Transcript-22)

#### Others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Others")]

In 2007, Peters was the first comedian to sell out Toronto's [Air Canada Centre](/wiki/Air_Canada_Centre "Air Canada Centre"), selling more than 16,000 tickets in two days for the single show. He ended up selling more than 30,000 tickets nationally over the two-day sales period. He broke a [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") comedy sales record at London's [O2 Arena](/wiki/O2_Arena_(London) "O2 Arena (London)") when he sold over 16,000 tickets to his show in 2009.[[23]](#cite_note-23) His show in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") on 15 May 2010 had an audience of 13,880, making it the largest stand-up comedy show ever in Australia.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Peters's performances on 5–6 May 2012 in Singapore also set attendance records for a single stand-up comedian at the [Singapore Indoor Stadium](/wiki/Singapore_Indoor_Stadium "Singapore Indoor Stadium").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Peters hosted the [Canada Day](/wiki/Canada_Day "Canada Day") Comedy Festival 2006, and participated in a [USO tour](/wiki/USO_tour "USO tour") of Iraq, Afghanistan, Germany, Africa and Greenland in November 2007 with [Wilmer Valderrama](/wiki/Wilmer_Valderrama "Wilmer Valderrama") and [Mayra Veronica](/wiki/Mayra_Veronica "Mayra Veronica").[[26]](#cite_note-BagramUSOTour20071121-26) He also produced and starred in the [radio](/wiki/Radio_comedy "Radio comedy") [situation comedy](/wiki/Situation_comedy "Situation comedy") series *Monsoon House* on [CBC Radio One](/wiki/CBC_Radio_One "CBC Radio One").

[![Peters hosting the Juno Awards 2009](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/JunoAwards2009-RussellPeters.jpg/250px-JunoAwards2009-RussellPeters.jpg)](/wiki/File:JunoAwards2009-RussellPeters.jpg)

Peters hosting the [Juno Awards 2009](/wiki/Juno_Awards_of_2009 "Juno Awards of 2009")

Peters was the host of the televised [2008 Juno Awards](/wiki/Juno_Awards_of_2008 "Juno Awards of 2008") ceremonies in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary") on 6 April 2008,[[27]](#cite_note-27) for which he won a [Gemini Award](/wiki/Gemini_Awards "Gemini Awards") for "Best Performance or Host in a Variety Program or Series."[[28]](#cite_note-28) The show received the second-highest ratings of any Juno Awards broadcast. Following the show's success, Peters accepted an invitation to host the [Juno Awards](/wiki/Juno_Awards "Juno Awards") for a second consecutive year; the [2009 Juno Awards](/wiki/Juno_Awards_of_2009 "Juno Awards of 2009") took place in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") on 29 March 2009.

He started his RELAX World Tour in November 2024 in the United States and subsequently performing in other parts of the world. The tour is anticipated to end in June 2025 with the last show in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 2025, Peters performed as a headliner in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia")'s [Riyadh Comedy Festival](/wiki/Riyadh_Comedy_Festival "Riyadh Comedy Festival"), an event taking place from September 26th to October 9th.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) The comedy festival overlaps with the seventh anniversary of [the assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_Jamal_Khashoggi "Assassination of Jamal Khashoggi") of [Jamal Khashoggi](/wiki/Jamal_Khashoggi "Jamal Khashoggi"), prompting Joey Shea, Saudi Arabia researcher at [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch"), to say in a statement that the Saudi government is using the comedy festival to [whitewash](/wiki/Whitewashing_(communications) "Whitewashing (communications)") its [human rights abuses.](/wiki/Human_rights_abuses "Human rights abuses")[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Peters has performed in the region before, having spoken of his experience performing for Saudi royalty in a 2017 episode of Comedy Central's series, *[This is Not Happening](/wiki/This_Is_Not_Happening_(TV_series) "This Is Not Happening (TV series)")*.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### DVDs and book

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: DVDs and book")]

[![Russell Peters in Afghanistan on a USO tour (November 2007)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Russell_Peters_in_Afghanistan_on_a_USO_tour_in_November_2007.jpg/250px-Russell_Peters_in_Afghanistan_on_a_USO_tour_in_November_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russell_Peters_in_Afghanistan_on_a_USO_tour_in_November_2007.jpg)

Russell Peters in Afghanistan on a [USO tour](/wiki/USO_tour "USO tour") (November 2007)

Peters released his debut comedy album, *[Outsourced](/wiki/Outsourced_(album) "Outsourced (album)")*, of his performance aired on [Comedy Central](/wiki/Comedy_Central "Comedy Central") on 26 August 2006. The [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") version is uncensored; it has sold more than 100,000 copies, and remained on the National DVD Chart over one and a half years after its release.

Peters released a second DVD/CD combo, *[Red, White and Brown](/wiki/Red,_White_and_Brown "Red, White and Brown")*, in Canada in 2008, and in the U.S. in early 2009. It was recorded on 2 February 2008, at the [WaMu Theatre](/wiki/WaMu_Theater_at_Madison_Square_Garden "WaMu Theater at Madison Square Garden") in New York City's [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"). It was self-produced and financed by Peters and his brother Clayton.

On 26 October 2010, Peters published his autobiography, *Call Me Russell*, co-written with his brother, Clayton, and Dannis Koromilas.

[![Peters at Canada's Walk of Fame 2011](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Russell_Peters-Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame_2011-15491.jpg/250px-Russell_Peters-Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame_2011-15491.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russell_Peters-Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame_2011-15491.jpg)

Peters at [Canada's Walk of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame "Canada's Walk of Fame") 2011

In May 2011, Peters released *The Green Card Tour: Live from the O2 Arena*, a live performance recorded in front of a total audience of 30,000, over two nights at [O2 Arena](/wiki/The_O2_(London) "The O2 (London)") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Also in 2011, Peters received a star on [Canada's Walk of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame "Canada's Walk of Fame").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Comedic style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Comedic style")]

Peters's stand-up performances feature [observational comedy](/wiki/Observational_comedy "Observational comedy"), using humour to highlight racial, ethnic, class and cultural stereotypes. He often refers to his own experiences growing up in an [Anglo-Indian](/wiki/Anglo-Indian_people "Anglo-Indian people") family, and impersonates the accents of various ethnic groups to poke fun at them. As he told an audience in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), "I don't make the stereotypes, I just see them."[[38]](#cite_note-38) In a 2006 interview with *[The National](/wiki/The_National_(CBC) "The National (CBC)"),* Peters observed that he did not intend to put down or offend different races and cultures, but tried to "raise them up through humour".[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Peters is widely known for his punchline, "Somebody gonna get a hurt real bad." It ends a joke he tells about his childhood with a traditional Indian father, who used [corporal punishment](/wiki/Corporal_punishment "Corporal punishment") on his sons. Another punchline he uses is "Be a man! Do the right thing!", which relates to a story of a Chinese man trying to get him to pay more for an item at a shop.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Peters lives in [Los Angeles, California](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California"), and owns two homes there. He also owns homes in [Las Vegas Valley, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Valley,_Nevada "Las Vegas Valley, Nevada"), and [Vaughan, Ontario](/wiki/Vaughan,_Ontario "Vaughan, Ontario").[[4]](#cite_note-Hough-4)

In 2010, Peters established the Russell Peters North Peel Scholarship, an award worth up to CA$21,000 and intended to finance up to three years of college.[[41]](#cite_note-morvay-41) It will be awarded annually to a student from [Judith Nyman Secondary School](/wiki/Judith_Nyman_Secondary_School "Judith Nyman Secondary School") (formerly North Peel) with a strong academic record and the intention of attending [college](/wiki/College "College").[[10]](#cite_note-Digitaljournal.com-10)[[41]](#cite_note-morvay-41)

Peters has been a practitioner of [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu") since 2015, and was promoted to purple belt by [Jean Jacques Machado](/wiki/Jean_Jacques_Machado "Jean Jacques Machado") in 2025.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Relationships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Relationships")]

Peters proposed to girlfriend Monica Diaz on 10 July 2010, at the [Los Angeles International Airport](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport") and announced their engagement via [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"). The couple married on 20 August 2010, at [A Little White Wedding Chapel](/wiki/A_Little_White_Wedding_Chapel "A Little White Wedding Chapel") in [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"). The wedding was attended by about 20 guests, including an [Elvis impersonator](/wiki/Elvis_impersonator "Elvis impersonator"). Soon after, Peters told *[The Canadian Press](/wiki/The_Canadian_Press "The Canadian Press")* that Diaz was pregnant, saying, "Did I get married because she was knocked up? I would say that expedited it."[[43]](#cite_note-pregnancymarriage-43) Their daughter, Crystianna Marie Peters, was born two months early on 14 December 2010.[[44]](#cite_note-fatherhood-44) In a March 2012 interview, Peters revealed that he and Diaz were divorcing.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-divorce-46)

In October 2016, it was announced that Peters was engaged to Ruzanna Khetchian.[[47]](#cite_note-47) After the engagement was called off, Peters announced on 4 December 2018, via [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), that he and his new girlfriend Jennifer Andrade were expecting a child.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Andrade was the Miss Universe Honduras in 2012. In April 2019 it was announced that Andrade had given birth to a boy, whom they named Russell Santiago Peters.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) His relationship with Andrade ended in 2020.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

On 20 February 2022, Peters married Ali Peters at the [Ritz Carlton](/wiki/Ritz_Carlton "Ritz Carlton") in [Dana Point, California](/wiki/Dana_Point,_California "Dana Point, California").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Religious beliefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Russell_Peters&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Religious beliefs")]

When interviewer [Larry King](/wiki/Larry_King "Larry King") asked Peters, "Is there such a thing as too taboo?", Peters replied, "I don't talk about religion because I think people are a little weird about religion, especially nowadays, and I'm more of a science guy than I am a beliefs guy. I'm more into facts than I am into beliefs."[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) Peters is an [atheist](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism").[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Peters, along with [Scot McFadyen](/wiki/Scot_McFadyen "Scot McFadyen"), [Rodrigo Bascuñán](/wiki/Rodrigo_Bascu%C3%B1%C3%A1n "Rodrigo Bascuñán"), [Shad](/wiki/Shad_(rapper) "Shad (rapper)"), [Sam Dunn](/wiki/Sam_Dunn "Sam Dunn"), and Darby Wheeler, at the [Peabody Awards](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award").

Russell Peters has appeared in many films. Earlier in his career, he had [cameo roles](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") in *Boozecan* (1994) as Snake's Friend, *Tiger Claws III* (2000) as Detective Elliott, *[My Baby's Daddy](/wiki/My_Baby%27s_Daddy "My Baby's Daddy")* (2004) as the obstetrician, and *Quarter Life Crisis* (2006) as Dilip Kumar.

He appeared in *[Senior Skip Day](/wiki/Senior_Skip_Day "Senior Skip Day")* (2008), which starred [Larry Miller](/wiki/Larry_Miller_(entertainer) "Larry Miller (entertainer)"), [Tara Reid](/wiki/Tara_Reid "Tara Reid"), and Gary Lundy. That year he was also in *[The Take](/wiki/The_Take_(2008_film) "The Take (2008 film)")* (2008) as Dr. Sharma.

He acted in the [Punjabi-Canadian](/wiki/Punjabi_Canadians "Punjabi Canadians") film *[Breakaway](/wiki/Breakaway_(2011_film) "Breakaway (2011 film)")* (2011), alongside [Rob Lowe](/wiki/Rob_Lowe "Rob Lowe"), [Camilla Belle](/wiki/Camilla_Belle "Camilla Belle"), [Anupam Kher](/wiki/Anupam_Kher "Anupam Kher"), and Vinay Virmani. That year he also acted in [Duncan Jones](/wiki/Duncan_Jones "Duncan Jones")'s *[Source Code](/wiki/Source_Code_(film) "Source Code (film)")* (2011) as Max, an amateur comedian with a bad attitude; and as Pervius in *[National Lampoon's 301: The Legend of Awesomest Maximus](/wiki/National_Lampoon%27s_301:_The_Legend_of_Awesomest_Maximus "National Lampoon's 301: The Legend of Awesomest Maximus")* (2011).[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Peters has guest-starred on the TV series *[Mr. D](/wiki/Mr._D "Mr. D")* as the school superintendent. In 2011, he starred in a Canadian TV Christmas special, *A Russell Peters Christmas*. Guests included [Michael Bublé](/wiki/Michael_Bubl%C3%A9 "Michael Bublé"), [Pamela Anderson](/wiki/Pamela_Anderson "Pamela Anderson"), and [Jon Lovitz](/wiki/Jon_Lovitz "Jon Lovitz"). The show attracted the most viewers of any CTV Canadian holiday special.
